title: BLD-527: Replace hlx self-update with GitHub Release assets
description: This tour walks through the complete replacement of the hlx update mechanism. The old approach ran 'npm install -g git+https://...#main', which triggered a TypeScript build (tsc) on the user's machine — failing when devDependencies were unavailable and bricking the CLI if the install failed mid-way. The new approach builds a prebuilt tarball in CI, publishes it as a rolling GitHub Release tagged 'latest', and installs via a staged download-validate-swap mechanism with rollback. The auto-tag workflow that chained every main merge to npm publish was also deleted.

steps.description: This entire workflow has been deleted. It previously fired on every push to main, read the version from package.json, and pushed a v{version} tag — which chained through publish.yml to trigger an npm publish. This created unwanted coupling: every merge to main risked an accidental npm publish and required a custom RELEASE_TOKEN secret. Removing it decouples CI builds from npm releases; publish.yml still exists and still triggers on v* tags pushed manually when a real npm release is intended.
steps.title: Deleted: auto-tag workflow

steps.description: This new workflow replaces auto-tag.yml. It triggers on push to main and builds a prebuilt tarball (helix-cli.tgz) containing dist/, skill-content/, package.json, and build-metadata.json — deliberately excluding src/ and test files. The tarball is published as a rolling GitHub Release under the tag 'latest' (not a v* tag, so publish.yml is never triggered). Using 'contents: write' permission with the built-in github.token avoids the RELEASE_TOKEN secret. The concurrency group ensures only one build-release runs at a time.
steps.title: New: build-release workflow

steps.description: The workflow runs 'npm ci' (which triggers the prepare script and runs tsc, building dist/) and then 'npm test' before creating the tarball. This guarantees the tarball always contains a tested, compiled artifact. The comment notes that the prepare script runs build automatically — this is why src/ is not needed in the tarball: the compiled output in dist/ is already present.
steps.title: npm ci + npm test before packaging

steps.description: A build-metadata.json file is generated and embedded in the tarball containing the commit SHA and build timestamp. This allows 'hlx --version' to surface the exact commit that produced the installed binary, satisfying acceptance criterion AC-9. The metadata file is created inline using GITHUB_SHA (available as an environment variable in all GitHub Actions runners).
steps.title: Build metadata injection

steps.description: The tarball explicitly excludes *.test.js and *.test.d.ts and does NOT include src/. This is the critical design decision: users installing from the tarball get prebuilt dist/ files only. The old 'npm install -g git+https://' path included src/ and triggered tsc via the prepare script, which failed when TypeScript was unavailable. With this tarball, no build step is needed on the user's machine.
steps.title: Tarball creation — no src/, no test files

steps.description: The workflow deletes any existing 'latest' release (with '|| true' to tolerate the first run where none exists), then recreates it pointing to the current commit SHA. This rolling release pattern means the update check in check.ts can always fetch a stable URL: the GitHub Releases API for tag 'latest' always returns the most recent main build. The tag 'latest' does not match the 'v*' glob in publish.yml's trigger, so npm publish is never accidentally invoked.
steps.title: Rolling 'latest' release — delete and recreate

steps.description: The diagnosis statement captures the three root causes addressed by this change: (1) source-based install requires tsc on user machines, (2) npm install -g is destructive with no staging or rollback, and (3) auto-tag.yml coupled every main merge to npm publish. The diagnosis drove the implementation plan: prebuilt tarball in CI eliminates cause 1, staged download-validate-swap eliminates cause 2, and deleting auto-tag.yml eliminates cause 3.
steps.title: Diagnosis: root causes documented

steps.description: The code review identified one correctness issue: the installation instruction in src/docs/cli-content.ts originally told users to run 'npm install -g ./' from the extracted tarball without --ignore-scripts. This would have triggered the prepare script (tsc), which fails because src/ is not in the tarball. The fix adds '--ignore-scripts' to the npm install command, ensuring users install the prebuilt dist/ files directly without any build step. This is the only post-implementation correctness fix.
steps.title: Code review: correctness fix for --ignore-scripts

steps.description: The code review confirms all 10 acceptance criteria are met after the --ignore-scripts fix: build-release.yml creates a GitHub Release without a v* tag (AC-1), auto-tag.yml is deleted (AC-2), publish.yml is unchanged (AC-3), hlx update uses the prebuilt artifact (AC-4), failed updates preserve the current install via backup/rollback (AC-5), auto-update uses the same staged mechanism (AC-6), auth failures provide explicit guidance (AC-7), no npm install -g git+https references remain (AC-8), hlx --version shows commit SHA (AC-9), and staged validation prevents bricking (AC-10).
steps.title: All 10 acceptance criteria satisfied

steps.description: The new release pipeline is self-contained and safe: every push to main produces a tested, prebuilt tarball published as a rolling 'latest' GitHub Release. The hlx update path in src/update/ downloads this tarball, extracts it to a staging directory, validates the staged install by running the entrypoint with HLX_SKIP_UPDATE_CHECK=1, and only then performs an atomic rename-based swap with a .bak backup and rollback on failure. The publish.yml workflow for npm releases remains completely unchanged and is only triggered by manually pushed v* tags.
steps.title: Summary: clean code paths confirmed
